title: Program Review for Social Sciences 18-19 (Part 1)
text:
Program Review for Social Sciences
Principal Preparer: Dr. Zachary Zweigle
Contributors: Chris Vancil, Dr. Andrea Craddock, Katie Gale, and Dr. Nathan Rexford
1. Description of Program
a. Organization: Dr. Zachary Zweigle, Dean of Instruction
Chris Vancil, History Instructor
Dr. Andrea Craddock, Psychology and Sociology Instructor
Katie Gale, Political Science Instructor
Erica Jameson, Part-time History Instructor
Dave Bush, Part-time History Instructor
Dr. Ahmed Ismail , Former Social Sciences Instructor
Rita-Jean Browne, Part-time Anthropology Instructor
Kim Freeze, Part-time Psychology Instructor
Shannon Pierce, Part-time Psychology Instructor
Heather Collins, Part-time Psychology Instructor
Jennifer Kalfsbeck-Goetz, Part-time Sociology Instructor
Peter Aguilera, Part-time Sociology Instructor
Sherry Ackermann, Part-time Social Sciences Instructor
b. Primary Purpose: To cultivate students in our perspective programs, prepare students
for transfer, and prepare students for life outside the College of the Siskiyous (COS).
c. Whom you serve: The students enrolled at the College of the Siskiyous. We serve about
40% of non-white students, which is reflective of the proportion of minority students we
have on campus. On average, social sciences department serves 56% of females, which
is higher than the college average of 48% females. We have at least 34% of our students
that come from local high schools, although data are insufficient in this area.
d. What kind of services you provide: We provide general education transferable courses,
as well as program-specific discipline courses. We also provide extracurricular activities
in the form of clubs, such as Psychology Club, History Club, and Board Gaming club.
e. How you provide them: Degree applicable courses, transfer courses
f. Describe how your curriculum is up-to-date and needs based. We have updated our
curriculum so that our courses are transferable to CSU, IGETCU, and other four-year
institutions. Psychology has added an Associates’ Degree for Transfer (ADT) to assist
students in transferring to four-year colleges. Faculty members are in the process of
submitting their courses for their three-year approval. This process has been hindered
slightly because of unexpected delays due to a transitioning of curriculum software.
New courses were added to fulfill ADT requirements in Psychology, History, and Political
Science.
g. Provide a breakdown of the classes offered in your areas within the Program Review
cycle.
Psychology – General Psychology (PSY 1001)
Social Psychology (PSY 1003)
Abnormal Psychology (PSY 1502)
Psychology of Prejudice (PSY 1004)
Research Methods (PSY 2001)
Understanding the Aging Brain of Older Adults (PSY 1070)

-- 1 of 4 --

title: Program Review for Social Sciences 18-19 (Part 2)
text:
Sociology - Introduction to Sociology (SOC 1001)
Social Problems (SOC 1002)
Earth Wisdom: Women, Nature, and the Environment (SOC 1032)
Anthropology – Introduction to Anthropology (ANTH 1010)
History – Western Civilization to 1600 (HIST 1005)
Western Civilization since 1600 (HIST 1006)
History of Women in the United States (HIST 1008)
United States History to 1877 (HIST 1017)
United States History since 1877 (HIST 1018)
Special Studies (HIST 2929)
World Civilization to 1450 (HIST 1009)
World Civilization since 1450 (HIST 1010)
American Military History (HIST 1023)
Eastern Civilizations since 1600 (HIST 1047)
Political Science – American Government: National, State, and Local (POLS 1001)
World Politics (POLS 1007)
Ethnic Studies – Ethnic Studies (ETHN 1001)
2. External Factors with Significant Impact: The requirements of the four-year institutions have
caused us to update our curriculum, create ADTs, and make sure that courses align with C-ID
expectations.
3. Progress on Outcomes Assessment
a. We did course-level SLO measures; however, in the midst of this Program Review we
changed the SLO process. Thus, we are in a “hiatus” in terms of creating and
implementing a SLO procedure. This year (18 – 19) is deemed the “initialization year” in
the SLO manual, so faculty are supposed to work with the SLO committee to create
program maps, make any necessary changes to program-level outcomes, and develop
their first key assessment (to be implemented next year). Unfortunately, there is no
currently-functioning SLO committee.
b. Chris Vancil added a lab component to HIST 1017 and HIST 1018. It has been noted by
this instructor that success rates have increased by 10 percent.
c. The Social Sciences department will create and implement a common assessment that
measures the program-level SLO.
4. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
a. Course Completion Rate - Our average rate of completion (retention) across the Social
Sciences classes are 78.7%. There are better completion rates to those disciplines where
there are current full-time faculty members teaching the courses.
b. Course Success Rate – Average rate of success across the Social Sciences courses are
68.1%, which meets the college’s stated goal of 65% successful student course

-- 2 of 4 --

title: Program Review for Social Sciences 18-19 (Part 3)
text:
completion. Again, higher success rates were noted for those disciplines where there is
a current full-time faculty member.
c. FT/PT Faculty Ratio- Approximately, 56% of part-time faculty taught social sciences
courses. In every major term, more courses were taught by part-time instructors
compared to full-time instructors.
5. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research
a. Of the Liberal Arts and Sciences (LAS) degree awards, the most awarded is the LAS with
a Social Sciences Emphasis degree.
b. 57% of these awards have a Social Sciences Emphasis. Completed AA degrees in
Psychology have grown in the last few years.
c. We have observed the majority of awards granted are LAS with Social Sciences Emphasis
degrees. This may make us rethink course offerings and ADT creation and
implementation.
6. Evaluation
a. Although we provide various modes and schedules of delivery for our courses, there are
some deficits that need to be addressed. Early morning classes and night classes
sometimes have low enrollment. Videoconference courses can be problematic when
there are less than five students enrolled in the distant classroom in terms of teaching
pedagogy.
b. Two of the three social science full-time faculty are in the tenure process and, thus,
receive student and peer evaluations each year that they use to refine and improve
their teaching practices.
c. We have lack of funding for resources the last couple of years. More specifically, we
thought we had funds available to purchase technology, classroom resources, and
professional development and these funds “disappeared” when we attempted to spend
them.
d. In terms of staffing needs, we need full-time sociology instructor to head that program.
As has been shown previously, the social sciences department accounts for 12% to 13%
of transfer-level credit courses. If we had a full-time sociology instructor, we could have
further growth in FTES in social sciences and for the college as a whole.
e. The full-time faculty are very engaged in committee work, including (but not limited to)
Academic Senate, Senate Executive Board, Student Learning Committee, Curriculum
Committee, Instruction Council, College Council, Faculty Association, Guided Pathways,
and Interstate Passport.
f. Professional development resources have been lacking in the social sciences
department. We have sought professional development resources, but they have never
been available.
g. College of the Siskiyous is part of the Online Educational Initiative (OEI) which allows us
to meet with Canvas experts that will improve our online courses. Dr. Craddock is in the
process of getting her general psychology course OEI certified and the other social
science faculty are following suit.
7. Vision – In the next four years, we envision having one or two more full-time faculty in social
sciences. Importantly, we need a full-time sociology instructor to develop a robust Sociology
program. We know the prospects of enhancing FTES in the Sociology program are favorable.
There is incredible growth potential for our Sociology program. This is the second year we are

-- 3 of 4 --

title: Program Review for Social Sciences 18-19 (Part 4)
text:
submitting a CQIP for a full-time sociology instructor. It was ranked by fellow faculty, College
Council, and Institutional Planning & Budget as the second highest faculty position that needs to
be filled, but it has not been funded.
Given the importance of social sciences in generating FTES, there should be more
consideration of course offerings and class schedules in order to meet our students’ needs. We
also hope that department funds will be available to gain access to professional development
and educational resources.
As we mentioned previously, many of our students are outside the county. To better
access these students, we need to pursue a full-bodied online course repertoire. We also
foresee that a few introductory online courses in the social sciences will be OEI certified and
available on the consortium.
8. Progress in Prior Goals – Since this is the inaugural year, we have no prior four-year action plan
upon which to reflect.
9. Four-year Action Plan –
1. We will maintain our retention and success rates over the course of the next four-year
cycle.
2. We will implement an ADT in political science by Spring 2020.
3. By the end of the four-year cycle, we will offer our courses within a two-year cycle as
required by the ADT in that discipline.
4. The Social Science department will have one OEI certified course in each discipline
represented by a full-time instructor by the next Program Review.
5. The Social Science department will create and implement a process to assess SLOs at the
program level within the SLO manual.
6. All full-time faculty members in the Social Science department will be tenured by the
next Program Review.
7. We will research the viability of a Social Work program for the College of the Siskiyous
by the next Program Review.

-- 4 of 4 --
